title: Seedling Rate
html:
The listings are not encouraging.

Most of what comes up under my search terms is either a role I'm overqualified for on paper or one that wants five years in a specialization I don't have. The ones in the middle, where I'd actually fit, all say "experience required" without specifying how much, which means the recruiter will decide that when they look at my CV. I apply to six in the first hour, copy-pasting my cover letter and adjusting the opening line each time, which is as much effort as the market deserves given the response rate I remember from the last time I did this.

By the end of the first day I have twelve applications out and a phone that doesn't ring.

Three days pass the way that kind of waiting always passes: slowly during the day and faster in retrospect. I eat from the convenience store two floors down and go for a ride in the evenings. The phone stays in my pocket and I check it too often. Two rejection emails arrive on day two. The rest of the applications stay in whatever folder sits between "received" and "we've decided to move forward with other candidates."

On the morning of the fourth day I go back into the void.

The system opens the same way it always does, the authority panel appearing as soon as I think about accessing it. I go straight to Life.

The numbers are different from when I left.

Planet 1's reading, which had been stuck at the fourth decimal place since I placed the first ground cover, now shows a legible figure: 0.0031 Life Energy. Three days of growth on minimum soil with a water deposit. The soil improvement timer is still running, 5 days remaining, so this is the pre-improvement rate. I look at the number for a moment. 0.0031 is not much. It is the first real return from anything I've put into this system.

Planet 2 shows 0.0008. Lower than Planet 1 because cold-adapted organisms generate slower and the planet's cycle runs at a slightly different duration. Planet 3 shows 0.0019, which is better than Planet 2 and close to Planet 1 despite having no water deposit, which confirms that the higher stability and better starting soil are doing what I expected.

Total across all three: 0.0058 Life Energy generated in 3 days. I add that to the 735 I came in with. The Life screen shows the combined figure: 735.0058. The fraction is almost invisible against the whole number, but it is above zero and it is moving in the right direction.

I check the Log.

---

[A void of nothingness.]

[Constellation manager appointed.]

[Terrain modification initiated on Planet 1.]

[Basic ground cover placed on Planet 1.]

[Basic grass placed on Planet 1.]

[Basic water deposit placed on Planet 1.]

[Cold-tolerant ground cover placed on Planet 2.]

[Cold-adapted moss placed on Planet 2.]

[Basic ground cover placed on Planet 3.]

[Basic grass placed on Planet 3.]

[Life Energy remaining: 735.0058.]

---

I close the Log and pull up Planet 2 in the surface view.

The moisture strip along the ridge is wider than it was when I left. The cold-adapted moss has spread along the wet ground, covering the darkest section of soil in a thin layer. It doesn't look like much from above but the Life counter confirms it's generating, and the conditions panel shows the moisture reading has ticked up slightly from the moss holding water at the surface.

The heat retention modification is still sitting in my cart at 80 Life Energy. The argument I made three days ago still holds: hold it until Planet 1's soil improvement completes and generation improves, then spend from the new income rather than the loan principal. 5 days until the soil improvement finishes. The generation rate I have now is 0.0058 per 3 days, which is roughly 0.002 per day. At that rate I'll have generated about 0.01 additional Life Energy by the time the soil improvement completes. Not 80. Not anywhere near 80.

The soil improvement finishing doesn't give me instant income. It raises the generation rate, which then compounds over subsequent cycles. The 80 LE for Planet 2's heat retention still has to come from the principal.

I spend 80. It brings me to 655.0058.

---

[Basic heat retention modification purchased. Applied to Planet 2. Effect: surface temperature raised by 1 band. Current temperature: temperate. Standard organism listings now available for Planet 2.]

---

I pull up Planet 2 again and check the conditions panel. Temperature now reads temperate instead of cold. The organism filter updates automatically, and the full listing opens up. Standard ground cover and basic grass are now accessible here, same as Planet 1 and Planet 3. The cold-tolerant organisms I already placed are still listed as active and will continue to generate. Adding the standard options on top gives me more coverage across the surface.

I buy basic grass and place it across the main flat zone away from the ridge, leaving the moisture strip area to the moss. The cost is 15 Life Energy, bringing me to 640.0058.

---

[Basic grass purchased. Items placed on Planet 2.]

---

640 remaining. Planet 1's soil improvement completes in 5 days. Planet 2 now running two organism types after the temperature fix. Planet 3 steady on medium soil with the best cycle speed.

I spend a few minutes in the surface view of Planet 3. Nothing has changed that I can see. The amber-grey sky sits above the darker brown-red soil, with the grass spread across the main zone in the same pattern I placed it. The Life counter shows it's the second-highest generator of the three planets. I haven't put a water deposit here yet. A basic water deposit costs 120, which I have, but adding it now cuts my remaining balance further. The generation from Planet 3 is already the best I have per organism placed. Water would improve it, and I check the conditions panel to see what the moisture-to-output relationship looks like.

The panel notes that moderate moisture enables standard growth but that above-moderate moisture raises growth rate by 15%. A basic water deposit on Planet 3's already-moderate soil would push moisture into the above-moderate range.

15% improvement on the best generator I have is more meaningful than 15% on either of the other two. I spend 120.

---

[Basic water deposit purchased. Applied to Planet 3.]

---

520.0058 Life Energy remaining.

I sit with that number. 520 is lower than I'd like. The loan started at 1,000 and I've spent 480 of it, plus whatever fraction of 1 I arrived with. The generation is running on all three planets now. Planet 1's soil improvement finishes in 5 days and once it does the rate goes up. Everything compounds from there.

The question I haven't fully answered is how long this takes to amount to anything. The loan grace period is 100 cycles at roughly 72 hours each. That's about 300 days. The repayment after that is 10% per cycle on the outstanding balance. If I've borrowed 1,000 and I'm spending it, I need the generation to be producing more than 100 per cycle before the grace period ends or the repayments eat into the principal faster than I'm generating.

0.002 per day is not 100 per cycle. Not by several orders of magnitude. I know that. The current rate is the seedling rate. The question is whether more life on the planets, placed under better conditions as I can afford them, pushes that number up into something that actually matters over 300 days.

I open Help and look for any entry that talks about generation scaling. There's a short paragraph near the bottom of the Life function description: Life Energy generation scales with the diversity and density of life present. Early-stage constellations generate very slowly. Sustained growth over multiple cycles compounds generation in a non-linear way.

Non-linear is either reassuring or something I should be worried about depending on which end of non-linear I end up on.

I close Help and exit.

***

Back in the room. The phone is on the desk where I left it. A notification sits in the top bar: one new email.

The subject line reads: Re: Application, Administrative Coordinator, Westing Group.

They want to schedule a phone screen.

I read the email twice. Westing Group is not a company I remember applying to, but I sent out twelve applications in the first hour on day one and the cover letters blur together. I check my sent folder and find it: day one, third application. Administrative Coordinator. The role description is broadly what I spent four years doing, which is presumably why they responded to this one and not the others.

I write back and suggest Thursday morning.

The phone screen doesn't solve rent in two weeks. It's a first step in a process that takes time I may not have in the right configuration. But it is something moving, and something moving is better than everything staying still.

I set the phone down and pull up the job listings again.
